game_data.title: United States v. Mercer — Medicare Fraud and Whistleblower Retaliation

suspect.name: Sylvia Mercer
suspect.role: CFO and Hospital Administrator, Ridgecrest Regional Medical Center
suspect.profile: Sylvia Mercer is a first-generation college graduate from rural Ohio who clawed her way through healthcare finance over two decades. She holds an MBA from Ohio State and built her reputation as a turnaround specialist for struggling regional hospitals, known for eliminating waste with surgical precision. Colleagues describe her as measured, deeply controlled, and intimidatingly competent—she rarely loses composure and speaks in the careful cadence of someone long accustomed to being recorded. Suspicious detail: she reformatted and wiped her hospital-issued laptop fourteen days before a federal compliance inquiry was formally announced, citing 'routine IT maintenance.' Humanizing detail: she has quietly and entirely funded her younger brother's residential addiction treatment for the past four years, telling no one at work and routing payments through a personal account.

system_config.fear_bar_description: Tracks cumulative psychological pressure on the suspect throughout the interrogation session. As the fear bar rises, the suspect's rehearsed composure erodes and the pharmacological suppression from propranolol and sertraline becomes progressively less effective at masking involuntary physiological responses. A high fear bar increases the probability of behavioral fractures, response latency spikes, and unguarded verbal admissions. A dropping or stalled fear bar indicates the suspect has re-established emotional control and may be moving toward a full defensive lockdown or counsel invocation.

game_data.context: The U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office of Inspector General has opened a federal investigation into Ridgecrest Regional Medical Center following a whistleblower complaint filed by former staff nurse Patricia Vance, who alleges that CFO Sylvia Mercer authorized a systematic Medicare upcoding scheme beginning in early 2023, fraudulently billing for higher-complexity procedures than were actually performed in order to artificially stabilize the hospital's failing financials. Vance further alleges that she was terminated without cause eleven days after submitting a formal internal compliance memo detailing the irregularities, constituting unlawful retaliation under the False Claims Act. Mercer denies all allegations, characterizing the billing adjustments as legitimate re-coding corrections reviewed by external consultants, and maintains that Vance's termination was a documented performance-based decision unrelated to the compliance filing. Investigators are scrutinizing a two-year period of Medicare claims totaling approximately $4.7 million in potential overbillings, as well as the destruction of Mercer's hospital-issued laptop shortly before the inquiry was announced. The case turns on whether Mercer personally directed the billing department to falsify codes or whether rogue subordinates acted without her authorization—a distinction that could mean the difference between a civil settlement and federal criminal charges.
game_data.verdict_truth_text: Sylvia Mercer personally directed the Medicare upcoding scheme by communicating with billing supervisor Marcus Webb through a secondary email account registered under her maiden name, Sylvia Crane—an account she created specifically to insulate herself from traceable authorization. She also signed nurse Patricia Vance's termination paperwork eleven days after Vance filed an internal compliance memo, and the performance justification documentation was fabricated after the fact to obscure the retaliatory timeline. Webb's cooperation agreement with federal investigators and his preserved local email archive provide direct documentary evidence of Mercer's personal direction of approximately $4.7 million in fraudulent Medicare claims.

dossier.identity_summary: Sylvia Mercer is the CFO and acting administrator of Ridgecrest Regional Medical Center, a 210-bed facility in central Pennsylvania. She holds an MBA and two healthcare compliance certifications and has led three prior hospital financial restructurings.

family.name: Derek Mercer
family.note: Financially dependent on Sylvia for ongoing residential addiction treatment; she becomes markedly destabilized when his situation is raised, making him high-value leverage.

family.name: Gerald Foss
family.note: Long-standing patron who appointed her to the CFO role; believed to have tacitly endorsed aggressive billing targets, giving Sylvia a potential shield—or a mutually assured destruction dynamic.

family.name: Thomas Crane
family.note: Contentious divorce finalized 2022; has stated publicly she 'had no moral ceiling where money was involved'—a potential hostile character witness whose credibility must be weighed.

medical.condition: Subclinical hyperthyroidism, diagnosed 2021, managed but not resolved
medical.polygraph_effect: Resting heart rate runs approximately 92–98 bpm; elevated baseline makes stress-induced cardiac spikes statistically harder to isolate as deception indicators.

medical.condition: Prescribed propranolol 40mg daily for hyperthyroid-related palpitations
medical.polygraph_effect: Beta-blocker substantially suppresses heart rate variability and cardiovascular reactivity; cardiac channel readings are significantly blunted and should be treated as low-confidence.

habits.habit: High caffeine consumption, 4–5 cups of coffee daily, consistent for years
habits.polygraph_effect: Chronically elevated GSR baseline; galvanic skin response is a poor deception signal in her case—elevated readings may reflect stimulant load rather than stress.

habits.habit: Sertraline 100mg daily (SSRI), prescribed for generalized anxiety, taken for six years
habits.polygraph_effect: Blunts sympathetic nervous system response broadly; overall polygraph reactivity is reduced across channels, increasing the risk of false-negative readings.

priors: 2019: Named as defendant in a wrongful termination civil suit filed by a former billing coordinator at her previous employer; settled out of court with confidential terms and no admission of liability.

2021: Resigned from Westfield Memorial Hospital mid-fiscal-year while an internal billing irregularity audit was ongoing; departure was framed as voluntary; audit records are partially sealed under a subsequent settlement.
No criminal record; no formal regulatory sanctions on file with state health department as of current date.
pressure_points: Derek's treatment dependency: any framing that implies her employment or income is at risk causes visible emotional fracturing—her affect shifts markedly and her prepared answers begin to unravel.
The Westfield 2021 departure: she has no clean or consistent explanation for the timing; pressing on specific dates and the audit's scope creates compounding timeline contradictions.

Nurse Vance's termination occurred eleven days after Vance filed an internal compliance memo—confronting Sylvia with that specific eleven-day gap produces measurable behavioral changes even through her pharmaceutical suppression.

node_01_intro.description: Sylvia Mercer sits across the table in a federal interview room, posture precisely controlled, hands folded. She has been informed this is a voluntary interview. Her attorney is not present—yet.

choices.question: Ms. Mercer, let's start with the hospital's financial picture when you came on board at Ridgecrest. What were the operating margins, and what did you identify as the primary drivers of loss?
choices.answer: She leans slightly forward, a flicker of confidence. 'Ridgecrest was running a negative four-point-two margin when I arrived. Uncompensated care was the dominant driver—roughly thirty-one percent of patient volume with no reimbursement pathway. CMS reimbursement rates hadn't kept pace with operational costs. It was a structural problem, not a management failure.' She's fluent, controlled, in her element.

mechanics.gameplay_note: BASELINE ESTABLISHED: Propranolol and sertraline both active—readings are suppressed across channels. This is her professional comfort zone. Use these numbers as your calibration reference for the rest of the session.

choices.question: Ms. Mercer, we're not here for pleasantries. You authorized a Medicare fraud scheme that cost taxpayers nearly five million dollars. Tell me exactly why you did it.
choices.answer: Her expression doesn't crack—it hardens. Three full seconds of silence. 'I authorized nothing illegal. If you have specific allegations supported by evidence, I'd recommend presenting them through proper channels to my attorney.' She places both palms flat on the table. 'I'm happy to cooperate professionally. But I won't sit here under an ambush framing. Shall I call counsel right now?'

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Aggressive cold-open triggers her threat-management protocol immediately. Propranolol blunts cardiac response—RISE is the ceiling here. Insufficient diagnostic data captured before she locks down.

node_02_financials.description: Sylvia has settled into a measured cadence, discussing hospital finances with practiced precision. The player must decide whether to press on the board's Q3 deadline or approach her financial desperation through softer empathic framing.

choices.question: The board issued a formal ultimatum—reach solvency targets by Q3 or face leadership replacement. That's in the board minutes. Given that pressure, walk me through every revenue-side option you evaluated to close the gap.
choices.answer: 'Gerald Foss and I discussed the full menu—vendor renegotiations, staffing right-sizing, a potential service-line consolidation. Revenue cycle optimization was on that list too, yes. We brought in an external coding consultancy.' A fractional pause—almost imperceptible—before adding: 'Their work was reviewed and approved internally.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: Propranolol suppresses cardiac channel—a SPIKE here is notable given that floor. EEG ERRATIC is the more reliable channel under her pharmaceutical profile. 'Reviewed and approved internally' is a rehearsed deflection inserted to preempt follow-up. PROGRESS.

choices.question: That must have been an impossible position—a board deadline, a hospital full of patients depending on you, and no clean way out. I can see why someone in that situation might feel they had no real options left.
choices.answer: Something genuine flickers across her face. Her jaw releases fractionally. 'You don't know what it's like to watch a community hospital bleed out and feel personally responsible for stopping it.' Then, more carefully: 'I did what I had to do to keep those doors open.' A half-beat pause. 'Through every legitimate avenue available to me.' She recalibrates—but the order of those sentences is already recorded.

mechanics.gameplay_note: PARTIAL TRUTH: 'I did what I had to do' preceded the qualifier—sequence matters. Sertraline blunts the full sympathetic cascade but EEG registers genuine emotional engagement. The self-correction is a tell. PROGRESS.

node_03_laptop.description: The laptop wipe is the clearest physical act of concealment in the record. Pressing it with documented forensics may force a contradiction; alternatively, widening the frame into a broader device-access question may reveal more.

choices.question: Your hospital-issued laptop was reformatted on March 3rd. The OIG inquiry was formally announced on March 17th. IT maintenance logs show no scheduled refresh cycle for that device in that window. Who authorized that wipe, and what was the specific justification you provided to IT?
choices.answer: 'IT handles its own scheduling. I submitted a general device refresh request—I was experiencing performance issues.' Her cadence slows almost imperceptibly. 'I don't track their maintenance calendar.' She holds eye contact a fraction too long before looking down.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: GSR SURGE is partially attributable to her caffeine-elevated baseline, but EEG ERRATIC in combination is significant. Cardiac SPIKE notable given propranolol floor. Over-held eye contact followed by look-down is a trained counter-tell losing ground. STRONG PROGRESS.

choices.question: Our forensic team recovered metadata from the hospital's network backup server showing a secondary device logged into the billing management system remotely seventeen times over a four-month period—outside business hours, under credentials not tied to your primary account. Did you ever access hospital systems from a personal device?
choices.answer: A full two-second silence. 'That would violate IT security policy. I don't—I've never used personal devices on secured hospital systems.' Her answer is technically narrow. She is not addressing what she knows; she is addressing only what she believes can be proven.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: MAX_SPIKE is extraordinary given propranolol suppression—indicates extreme acute stress. EEG CHAOTIC is the most reliable channel in her profile. Two-second pre-response pause is significant. Note the hedge 'secured hospital systems'—deliberate scope narrowing. STRONG PROGRESS to node_05.

node_04_vance_gap.description: Nurse Patricia Vance filed her internal compliance memo on November 4th. Sylvia signed Vance's termination paperwork on November 15th. That eleven-day gap is the spine of the retaliation allegation—pressing it precisely may produce a timeline contradiction.

choices.question: Patricia Vance submitted her internal compliance memo on November 4th. Her termination was countersigned by you on November 15th. That is eleven days. I need you to tell me, precisely, when you first read or were informed of the contents of that compliance memo.
choices.answer: Her hands press flat against the table. 'Compliance submissions are routed through HR. I don't receive them directly.' A short pause. 'I was presented with documented performance deficiencies regarding Nurse Vance in—' she selects her words— 'the late October timeframe. The decision was already in process.' She is building a firewall using dates she does not fully control.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: Strongest cardiac reading of the session—MAX_SPIKE despite propranolol suppression. EEG CHAOTIC. The 'late October' claim is contradicted by HR records showing zero performance documentation before November 8th. This is a provable lie. CRITICAL PROGRESS to node_05.

choices.question: Patricia Vance spent fifteen years as a floor nurse. She wasn't looking for a lawsuit—she was trying to protect patients. Whatever happened at Ridgecrest, don't you think she deserved better than to lose her job for telling the truth?
choices.answer: Whatever warmth briefly surfaced has completely disappeared. 'Nurse Vance's employment status was determined by documented performance criteria—not by the content of any internal memo I may or may not have been aware of. I'd recommend reviewing the HR file carefully before making characterizations.' A measured pause. 'I think at this point I should have my attorney present for any further questions.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Moral framing triggers trained professional detachment—she has rehearsed this exact deflection. EEG DROP indicates cognitive suppression response, not deception. Sertraline blunts emotional reactivity. Suspect is now requesting counsel. ROUTE TO FAIL.

node_05_email_trap.description: Billing supervisor Marcus Webb has cooperated with federal investigators and preserved a complete email archive. This is the most direct evidence in the case. How it is introduced will determine whether Sylvia cracks or calcifies entirely.

choices.question: Ms. Mercer, Marcus Webb has entered a formal cooperation agreement with federal investigators. He preserved a complete archive of emails exchanged with an account registered to a 'Sylvia Crane.' That account was created nine months before the billing irregularities began. I want to give you the opportunity to explain those communications before this session ends.
choices.answer: Silence. Four full seconds. Her breathing becomes audible. 'I...' She stops. Begins again. 'Any communications I may have used a personal account for were—were related to billing review processes that were legitimate at the time.' Her voice loses its practiced flatness entirely. 'I would need to see specifically what you're referring to.' Then, barely audible: 'I need to speak with my attorney before I say anything further.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL — MAXIMUM CLUSTER: All channels breach suppression thresholds simultaneously. Even with propranolol, sertraline, and elevated caffeine baseline, every channel hits its ceiling. This is diagnostically extraordinary given her pharmaceutical profile. The implicit acknowledgment that she knew the account being referenced is the closest to admission available without a formal confession.

choices.question: Ms. Mercer, you are facing criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1347. This is no longer a civil audit. Every moment you protect the people above you in this chain, you alone are the one looking at a federal indictment. Your brother Derek needs you available. Talk to me now, before this closes.
choices.answer: Complete stillness. Her face becomes entirely unreadable. 'I would like my attorney present. I have nothing further to say.' She places both hands in her lap, fixes her gaze on the wall, and goes silent. The Derek reference produces a single involuntary blink—a flash of something raw—then absolute nothing. Full shutdown.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Legal threat combined with personal leverage on Derek produced one detectable blink, then complete cognitive shutdown. EEG FLATLINE indicates dissociative withdrawal, not a deception signal—it cannot be used diagnostically. Insufficient channel depth for verdict-quality evidence. ROUTE TO FAIL.

node_success_partial_admission.description: Sylvia Mercer has invoked counsel following a response that implicitly acknowledged the existence of the secondary email account. The interrogation is suspended. The session produced a maximum-threshold biometric cluster at its peak moment.
node_success_partial_admission.result_text: Sylvia Mercer requested legal representation mid-response after partially addressing the Sylvia Crane account. She did not deny the account by name. Her final voluntary statement—'Any communications I may have used a personal account for were related to billing review processes that were legitimate at the time'—implicitly confirms she understood which account was being referenced without being shown documentation. Biometric data captured a simultaneous maximum-threshold reading across heart rate, GSR, and EEG at the moment the account name was introduced. Given her active propranolol and sertraline suppression, plus a chronically elevated caffeine GSR baseline, breaching all three channels simultaneously is diagnostically significant and cannot be attributed to pharmacological noise alone. No formal admission was recorded. The player must now assess whether the signal cluster, the implicit account acknowledgment, and the session's cumulative evidence pattern constitute sufficient grounds for a verdict determination.

node_fail_lockdown.description: Sylvia Mercer has invoked her right to counsel and ceased all substantive responses. The voluntary session is over. Signal data from the truncated session is limited, fragmented, and heavily filtered by her pharmacological profile.
node_fail_lockdown.result_text: Sylvia Mercer formally invoked her right to counsel and provided no further responses. No partial admissions were recorded. Biometric data collected before termination is incomplete—the session ended before the highest-yield questioning sequences were deployed, and the combined effect of propranolol, sertraline, and a caffeine-elevated GSR baseline means the partial readings captured lack the corroborating multi-channel depth needed for strong diagnostic confidence. Isolated RISE and INCREASE readings are present on several channels during the financial and personnel questions, but no sustained deception cluster was established. The player must now assess whether the fragmentary signals captured, combined with the pre-interrogation dossier and case record, provide sufficient evidence to support a verdict determination—or whether the evidentiary picture remains too ambiguous to call with confidence.
